92
   E.g., Cauchy, Du Duel.
93
   Soeur Mélanie c. Fougère [cite]; Sergent c. Defonds, Annales de la Propriété Industrielle, Artistique et
Littéraire 6 (1860), 168-171; Félix c. O’Connell, Annales de la Propriété Industrielle, Artistique et
Littéraire 4 (1858), 250-52. The beauties were Rachel, a famous tragic actress, and a certain Mlle. Sergent,
who, to judge by the opinion, lived something of a loose but exciting life. The missionary was Soeur
Rosalie. The graves of both Rachel and Soeur Rosalie are still visited today by Paris cemetery tourists. For
general discussion, see Vaunois in Annales de la Propriété Industrielle, Artistique et Littéraire 1888, 286.

31
 tend to do. Especially beginning in the gay years of the 1860s, “right to one’s image”

cases began to multiply.

A particularly famous 1867 case involved Alexandre Dumas père, the author of

The Three Musketeers, itself an important document of nineteenth-century French

dueling culture. Dumas père, then well on in years, became involved in a love affair with

Adah Isaacs Menken, a thirty-two- year-old Texas actress and horsewoman, famous for

appearing on stage dressed only in a body stocking. Obviously reveling in their rejection

of bourgeois values, the scandalous Menken and Dumas posed (together with Menken’s

mother!) for several more or less salacious photographs. Some of them showed Menken

in her underwear. Others showed her in amorous poses with Dumas, who was not

wearing a jacket. Dumas, whether through inadvertence or sheer glorious indifference,

did not enter into any express agreement with the photographer about the rights to publish

the photos. Seizing his chance, the photographer tried to register his copyright in what

were highly marketable images. In responding to the photographer’s application, the

copyright officials were not entirely without delicacy: They forbade him to display the

photos showing Miss Menken in her underwear. 94 With regard to the other photos,

though, they gave the photographer free rein, and he marketed them widely, causing an

international scandal.

Dumas, perhaps under pressure from his family, sued. 95 But could any objection

be raised in law? This was a difficult question in the 1860s. The photographer had a

property right, the copyright in the photographs. Indeed, Dumas admitted in open court

94
   Alexandre Dumas c. Liébert, in Annales de la Propriété Industrielle, Artistique et Littéraire 13 (1867):
248.
95
   Such is the account, at least, of Wolf Makowitz, Mazeppa: The Lives, Loves and Legends of Adah Isaacs
Menken (New York: Stein and Day, 1982), 177-178. For a somewhat different account, see Bernard Falk,
The Naked Lady, or Storm over Adah (London: Hutchinson, 1934), 199-203.

32
 that he had sold the rights. 96 This was the mid-nineteenth century, and property rights

were generally regarded as something close to sacred in the legal cosmos of the day.
